lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market mills repeatedly supplying external customers with nonwoven fabrics, felts, and further-fabricated nonwoven products made by mechanical, chemical, thermal, solvent, or combined bonding processes.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, diaper and medical-supply manufacturers classified downstream, and firms without repeat external-customer supply relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B sale of engineered roll goods, sheets, felts, or converted nonwoven components by specification, volume, production run, or supply agreement to medical/PPE, wipes, filtration, construction, industrial, and other downstream manufacturers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation evidence is for broader 3132 Fabric Mills, not a wage-weighted 313230 task inventory. The ten largest 3132 occupations include 2,450 synthetic/glass-fiber extruding and forming operators, 2,420 industrial machinery mechanics, 2,370 inspectors/testers, 2,330 production supervisors, 1,020 material movers, and 1,000 shipping/receiving clerks, alongside weaving roles less relevant to nonwovens. I infer exposure in process monitoring, visual inspection support, maintenance diagnostics, production planning, specification handling, quality documentation, procurement, sales, and administration. Broader-industry aggregation can bias either way; counting existing continuous-line automation as new AI would bias upward, so it is excluded.
a.rationale: Nonwoven plants are capital- and process-intensive with considerable physical production, maintenance, laboratory, material-handling, and safety work. AI can support sensor analytics, defect detection, recipe and yield optimization, maintenance, and information workflows, but most line operation and accountable quality decisions remain human- and equipment-dependent.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-and-time study across spunbond, meltblown, needle-punch, wet-laid, and bonded 313230 mills, separating current control-system automation from AI-addressable hours in process engineering, inspection, maintenance, planning, laboratory, sales, and administration.
caveats: Six-digit occupation employment is unavailable, and 3132 includes woven and knit processes.
The estimate covers not-yet-automated tasks only; current PLC, DCS, vision, and continuous-line automation are baseline.
Product and process mix can shift the exposed share materially between commodity roll goods and engineered specialty media.

rho.rationale: Continuous-process data and high material throughput create implementable analytics and inspection use cases, while specification extraction and back-office tools are comparatively straightforward. Deployment is constrained by legacy control integration, sparse labeled defect data, qualification requirements, cybersecurity, false-reject and off-spec risk, and small technical teams. Census manufacturing research reports any AI at 22.8% of establishments but production using AI at 8.0%, with cost cited as a barrier by 43.2%.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor and mill implementation cohorts for machine vision, predictive maintenance, process set-point recommendations, scheduling, quality documentation, and ERP copilots, with go-live, sustained-use, scrap, downtime, labor-hour, and customer-qualification outcomes over at least two years.
caveats: Manufacturing adoption data are not specific to nonwovens and include larger establishments.

A 2024 recordable injury incidence rate of 2.2 per 100 full-time workers at nonwoven fabric mills reinforces the need for controlled operational change but does not directly measure AI implementation.

e.rationale: Nonwoven mills ordinarily sell physical intermediate goods repeatedly to external customers, but process lines are capital-intensive and many facilities may belong to large strategic groups, serve captive downstream operations, depend on proprietary technology, or sit outside the target normalized EBITDA band. The frozen n=93 uses a food-processing margin bridge that may be particularly misleading for a capital-intensive specialty-material industry, so eligibility is discounted without re-estimating n.
e.change_evidence: A full 313230 establishment-to-ultimate-owner roster with line capacity and technology, captive versus external shipments, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, repeat-order revenue, and carve-out feasibility.
caveats: Eligibility is applied to frozen n=93 and does not replace that dataset input.
The frozen firm-count estimate relies on a margin proxy from a different manufacturing subsector.
Establishments and independently transferable firms may differ sharply in multi-plant materials groups.

s5.bridge: Census 2021 ABS research reports 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2020, not the age or transaction rate of all 313230 owners. I translate broad succession pressure into a 3%-6% annual qualifying-control-transfer hazard compounded across five years, then discount for strategic-group ownership, family/internal transfers, closures, and asset-only sales. Women-owned broad manufacturing may bias the estimate in either direction, and treating age as a completed sale would bias upward.
s5.rationale: Aging manufacturing owners create potential succession supply, but expensive specialized lines, large-company ownership, proprietary process know-how, environmental liabilities, customer qualification, and buyer concentration can reduce external control transfers in the target band.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, CapIQ, and industry-broker transactions matched to 313230 owners and facilities over ten years, classifying strategic group sales, sponsor control deals, family transfers, closures, minority investments, and asset-only liquidations separately.
caveats: The source is women-owned manufacturing across all subsectors, not nonwoven mills.
Owner age does not establish that a business is independently transferable or that a control sale will close.

q.rationale: Engineered specifications, customer qualification, yield performance, and supply reliability can let an operator retain benefits through margin, quality, or throughput rather than immediate price cuts. Large buyers, resin and fiber indexing, competitive rebids, import alternatives, and competitor adoption create pass-through. BLS reports 313230 output prices up 1.0% from September 2024 to September 2025 after a 1.8% decline in the prior twelve months, evidence of price movement but not of AI-benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and invoice-level contribution-margin cohorts around implemented AI, separated by cost-indexed, fixed-price, formula, and competitive-bid arrangements, with renewal pricing, rebate clauses, throughput, scrap, and win/loss data.
caveats: PPI is an output-price measure, not a measure of contractual pass-through or retained AI value.
Retention varies between commodity hygiene substrate and qualified filtration, medical, or industrial media.

d5.bridge: USITC trade values and application evidence are different quantities from five-year constant-price, constant-quality US demand. In 2021, nonwoven fabrics were the largest category within $5.5 billion of US fabric exports and were critical PPE inputs; in 2022, nonwoven imports fell $146 million, or 7.1%, as PPE demand normalized. EPA also documents nonwoven geotextile use where water passes through erosion-control devices. I treat the post-pandemic decline as normalization against a diversified medical, wipes, filtration, construction, and industrial base. Nominal trade values, imports rather than total consumption, and exceptional PPE vintages can bias both upward and downward, so the range is wide.
d5.rationale: Nonwovens serve multiple recurring physical functions and include higher-value engineered materials, reducing dependence on apparel alone. Secular filtration, wipes, hygiene, medical, geotextile, and industrial uses support modest growth, while PPE normalization, plastic-reduction pressure, recycling challenges, import competition, substitution, and capacity cycles create downside.
d5.change_evidence: INDA North American Nonwovens Supply Report capacity and tonnage by process and end use, USITC DataWeb quantity series by nonwoven HTS code, Census product-class shipments, and five-year customer forecasts for hygiene, wipes, filtration, medical, construction, automotive, and industrial media.
caveats: Trade values are nominal and do not directly measure US constant-quality demand.
The 2021-2022 period was distorted by pandemic PPE demand and normalization.
End markets and nonwoven processes have materially different growth, qualification, and substitution profiles.

o.rationale: Year-five demand is for physical bonded or interlocked fiber structures and felts. Software can automate ordering, design support, monitoring, and planning, but an accountable operator must own and run process lines, manage materials and recipes, meet tensile, porosity, filtration, cleanliness, and other specifications, inspect output, and deliver product.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and material-substitution studies quantifying planned self-manufacture, direct import, paper/film/woven substitution, reusable-product adoption, and any process technology that eliminates an independent nonwoven-mill operator.
caveats: The operator-required share is estimated rather than directly surveyed.
Elimination through reusable alternatives or material regulation belongs primarily in d5 and is not double counted here.

narrative.executive_view: Nonwoven mills supply repeat, specification-driven physical materials across diverse end markets. Their continuous-process data can support AI in inspection, maintenance, process optimization, planning, and administration, but the low compensation intensity and already automated equipment constrain the size of the labor opportunity.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can detect surface and formation defects, relate sensor histories to downtime or scrap, support recipe and set-point decisions, forecast production and raw-material needs, extract customer specifications, draft quality records, and automate routine service and back-office work. It does not replace extrusion, web formation, bonding, material handling, laboratory validation, maintenance, safety, or final release accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Qualified engineered products may retain benefits through better yield, consistency, uptime, and lead time, while commodity substrates face indexed inputs, rebids, imports, and price competition. Commercial evidence does not directly reveal retained AI benefits, so the range remains judgmental.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset supplies 93 candidate-band firms, but its food-processing margin bridge is weak for capital-intensive nonwovens. Large-group ownership, captive facilities, proprietary lines, and carve-out complexity likely reduce eligibility. Broad manufacturing age evidence suggests succession pressure but not a high observed deal rate.
narrative.demand_durability: Nonwovens are essential physical inputs in PPE and medical products, wipes, filtration, geotextiles, and industrial applications. Pandemic demand has normalized, as shown by the 2022 import decline, but diversified applications support a modest-growth base case with wide downside and upside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: No public six-digit occupation, AI adoption, or qualifying-transfer series was found. Evidence relies on broader Fabric Mills jobs, manufacturing-wide AI adoption and owner age, nominal trade flows, and end-use examples. Legacy controls, off-spec losses, customer validation, resin volatility, environmental regulation, capacity additions, and product-level cyclicality can overwhelm modeled gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Diversified, specification-critical physical demand paired with data-rich continuous processes that can support AI-assisted quality and uptime.
narrative.principal_weakness: Low labor intensity and already automated production limit labor capture, while the frozen firm count may overstate independently transferable targets.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Manufacturing Classification Form for Fabric Mills
sources.supports: Identifies 313230 as nonwoven fabric mills, including nonwoven felts and products further manufactured from nonwoven fabric, excluding downstream diapers and medical supplies.

sources.title: May 2024 OEWS largest occupations by industry
sources.supports: For broader Fabric Mills, reports large physical occupations including 2,450 fiber extruding/forming operators, 2,420 industrial machinery mechanics, 2,370 inspectors/testers, 2,330 production supervisors, and material-handling and shipping roles.

sources.title: The Rise of Industrial AI in America
sources.supports: Manufacturing-establishment evidence reports 22.8% with any AI, 8.0% with production using AI, 43.2% citing cost as a barrier, and 12.3% citing lack of expertise.

sources.title: 2024 nonfatal occupational injury and illness incidence rates by industry
sources.supports: Reports a total recordable case incidence rate of 2.2 per 100 full-time workers for Nonwoven Fabric Mills in 2024.

sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age
sources.supports: Reports that 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2020 and documents high older-owner shares in capital-intensive sectors.

sources.title: September 2025 Producer Price Index Detailed Report
sources.supports: Reports 313230 output prices up 1.0% from September 2024 to September 2025; the prior September report showed a 1.8% twelve-month decline.

sources.title: USITC Trade Shifts 2021: Textiles and Apparel
sources.supports: Reports $5.5 billion of US fabric exports in 2021, with nonwovens the largest fabric export category and critical inputs for N95 respirators, surgical masks, and other medical PPE.

sources.title: USITC Trends in U.S. Merchandise Trade, 2022
sources.supports: Reports nonwoven-fabric imports fell $146 million, or 7.1%, in 2022 as PPE demand decreased after COVID restrictions relaxed, and identifies facemasks, wipes, and medical supplies as uses.

sources.title: EPA Stormwater Best Management Practice: Geotextiles, Matting and Netting
sources.supports: Explains that nonwoven geotextiles are made by bonding fibers and are used where water passes through erosion- and sediment-control devices, including storm-drain inlet protection.
